title: Dynamical generation of stable optical-microwave squeezing in structured reservoirs
authors: Chen Wang, Man Shen, Shi-fan Qi, Yan-kui Bai
abstract: Two-mode squeezed states as paradigmatic entangled resources have broad applications in quantum information processing. Here, we study the generation of stable optical-microwave squeezing in structured environments within a hybrid electro-optomechanical system, where a mechanical oscillator is simultaneously coupled to an optical cavity mode and a microwave mode of an LC resonator. Specifically, an effective Hamiltonian that captures the optical-microwave squeezing interaction is constructed by combining strongly modulated driving fields applied to both photonic modes with a mechanical parametric amplifier. Based on this effective model, the dynamical evolution of two-mode squeezing in structured environments is analyzed. It is remarkably shown that the non-Markovian noise can substantially enhance the squeezing level in comparison to the Markovian case, and that two-mode squeezing can persist even in the absence of external driving fields under non-Markovian conditions, thereby mitigating the detrimental effects of anti-squeezing. Furthermore, the persistence of the two-mode squeezed state is enhanced when the environmental spectral densities of the microwave and optical modes are identical. Our work provides a theoretical framework for generating and persisting two-mode squeezing in structured environments.
summary: This paper investigates how structured, non-Markovian environments can be used to generate and stabilize two-mode squeezing between optical and microwave modes in a hybrid electromechanical system. The authors demonstrate that non-Markovian noise can enhance squeezing levels compared to standard Markovian noise and even allow the squeezed state to persist after the external driving fields are turned off. They also find that matching the spectral densities of the optical and microwave reservoirs is key to preventing the degradation of the squeezed state. This work offers a way to mitigate the detrimental effects of anti-squeezing in quantum transducers.
main_problem: The challenge of generating and maintaining stable, high-level optical-microwave two-mode squeezed states (TMSS) in the presence of noise and the limitations of Markovian environments.
main_result: Non-Markovian (structured) environments can significantly enhance squeezing levels and allow TMS to persist even after external driving is switched off, especially when the spectral densities of the two modes are matched.
method: The authors use second-order perturbation theory to derive an effective Hamiltonian and employ the Non-Markovian Hierarchical Equation of Motion (NMHL) to analyze the system's dynamical evolution.
model_or_system: A hybrid electro-optomechanical (EOM) system consisting of an optical cavity mode, a microwave LC resonator, and a mechanical oscillator, all coupled to structured (Lorentzian) reservoirs.
key_observables: Two-mode squeezing level (SL), squeezing variance (Delta X), and anti-squeezing variance (Delta Y).
important_parameters: Effective coupling strength (g_eff), mechanical parametric amplification (r), spectral density width (lambda), and detunings.
main_assumptions: The system operates in a large detuning regime where the effective Hamiltonian is valid, and the use of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) for system-reservoir coupling.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the EOM system; Fig 2: Time evolution of squeezing and anti-squeezing; Fig 3: Parameter space analysis of squeezing level; Fig 4: Squeezing persistence during drive switch-off; Fig 7-9: Validation of the effective Hamiltonian and coupling strength.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a system schematic and effective Hamiltonian derivation, followed by a numerical validation of the model, an analysis of squeezing dynamics in Markovian vs. non-Markovian environments, an investigation of squeezing persistence after driving is removed, and concludes with a comparison of matched vs. mismatched spectral densities.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical framework for using reservoir engineering and non-Markovian memory effects to enhance and stabilize entanglement, which is crucial for quantum transduction and continuous-variable quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram: The mechanical resonator serves as an intermediate mode b, coupling simultaneously to the microwave res- onator c and optical cavity a. The optical and microwave modes are driven by strong external ﬁelds Ea and Ec, respectively, while the mechanical mode is subjected to a nonlinear driving with amplitude Ωb, giving rise to mechanical parametric ampliﬁcation.

FIG. 2. (a) Time evolution of the squeezing variance ∆X(t) for dif- ferent environments and coupling strengths g. [(b), (c)] Time evo- lution of the anti-squeezing variance ∆Y (t) at g = 0.15˜ωb and g = 0.2˜ωb for different environments. The Markovian case is char- acterized by κo, whereas the structured environment is labeled by Jo. Here we ﬁx ∆c = 3.5˜ωb, α = ϕ = 0, g = G, and r = 0.2 for all ﬁgures. The parameters are chosen as γc = 1.5γa with γa = 10−3˜ωb and λc = 1.5λa with λa = 10−2˜ωb, while the ther- mal occupation numbers are ¯na = ¯nc = 0.

FIG. 3. [(a), (b)] The squeezing level (SL) S(τ) at ˜ωbτ = 300 is plotted in the parameter space spanned by the coupling strength g and the MPA parameter r for both Markovian and structured envi- ronments, respectively. [(c),(d)] The SL S(τ) is shown as a function of the global dissipation rate γa and spectrum width λa for vari- ous coupling strengths, respectively. In panels (c) and (d), we take g = G = 0.15˜ωb and r = 0.2. The other parameters are identical to those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. [(a), (b)] The time evolutions of the variances ∆X(t) and ∆Y (t) are shown for both the generation (teal areas) and persistence (beige areas) processes under different environmental noises, respec- tively. Here, the spectral-density parameters satisfy λc/λa = γc/γa, and all other parameters are identical to those in Figs. 2(b), except for γc and λc.

FIG. 5. The squeezing level S(T ) at a uniformly chosen prolonged duration T (with ˜ωbT = 1000) is plotted as a function of the driving- switch-off time τ. In panel (a), g = G, and all other parameters are identical to those in Fig. 2(b). In panel (b), g = G = 0.15˜ωb, γa = 10−3˜ωb, λa = 10−2˜ωb, and the ratio λc/λa = γc/γa. In panels (c) and (d), g = G = 0.15˜ωb and λc/λa = γc/γa = 1, while all other parameters remain unchanged.

FIG. 6. All evolution paths between the quantum states |nlk⟩and |(n + 1)l(k + 1)⟩are shown, with blue solid lines representing connections established by the optical-mechanical interaction and red dashed lines indicating transitions mediated by the mechanical- microwave interaction.

FIG. 8. [(a), (c)] Theoretical results about the effective coupling strength geﬀfrom Eq. (6) (lines) are plotted as functions of g and G, respectively, and compared against the numerical results, repre- sented by discrete points. [(b), (d)] Analytical results for the energy shift δ in Eq. (6) (lines) are similarly compared with the numeri- cally obtained values (points) as functions of g and G, respectively. G = 0.15˜ωb for panels (a) and (b), and g = 0.15˜ωb for panels (c) and (d). Additionally, ∆c = 3.5˜ωb is ﬁxed for all panels.

FIG. 7. Two relevant imaginary components of the normalized eigen- values of the transition matrix are plotted versus the detuning fre- quency ∆a. The parameters used are ∆c = 3.5˜ωb, g = G = 0.15˜ωb, and r = 0.2.

FIG. 9. The dynamics of the variance ∆X(t) are presented using the effective Hamiltonian in Eq. (5) and the full system Hamiltonian in Eq. (4). The inset depicts the evolution of the corresponding CM element under the effective and full Hamiltonians. The parameters are set as κb = 10−5˜ωb, κa = 0.002˜ωb, κc = 10−3 ˜ωb, and the thermal numbers Na = Nc = 0 and Nb = 10. Other parameters are the same as Fig. 7.
